title: Jeremiah 20
subtitle: Pashhur and the Final Confession

timeline_link.text: See on Timeline — Pashhur Beats Jeremiah

heb.paragraph: Jeremiah renames Pashhur "Magor-Missabib" — "terror on every side" (v.3). This is the first of Jeremiah's renaming oracles, a prophetic act that redefines a person's identity according to their destiny. Pashhur, whose name may mean "ease/prosperity," will become the embodiment of terror. The phrase "terror on every side" becomes a signature expression in Jeremiah (6:25, 20:10, 46:5, 49:29).

refs.note: The apostles are flogged by the Sanhedrin — the pattern of religious authorities persecuting prophets continues into the NT.
Pashhur's successor Zephaniah is urged to put Jeremiah in stocks again — the institutional hostility persists across administrations.

notes.note: Pashhur the priest — not a false prophet but a temple official — has Jeremiah beaten and placed in stocks. The persecution comes from the religious establishment, not from secular authorities.

The renaming: Pashhur becomes "Terror on Every Side." The oracle specifies the terror: Pashhur will watch his friends die by the sword, Judah will be exiled to Babylon, and the temple treasures will be plundered.
Pashhur himself will go to Babylon and die there — he and "all who live in your house." The false peace he preached will be his ruin. He prophesied lies (šeqer), and the lies will consume him.

Calvin observes that persecution by religious leaders is the most painful form of opposition: "When the temple itself becomes the instrument of persecution, the prophet has nowhere left to turn except to God.". Calvin's reading characteristically emphasizes divine sovereignty and human responsibility in tension — God ordains the outcome while the actors remain morally accountable. This dual emphasis reflects Calvin's broader hermeneutic: Scripture always speaks both about God's plan and about human duty under that plan.

netbible.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: The mahpeket ("stocks") was a device that confined the body in a cramped, contorted position — not merely restraint but torture. The upper Benjamin Gate was a prominent, public location, maximizing the humiliation.
The name change from Pashhur to Magor-Missabib functions as a prophetic performative act — the renaming enacts the judgment it announces.

lundbom.source: Jack R. Lundbom, Jeremiah, Anchor Bible (3 vols., 1999–2004) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Lundbom notes that the Pashhur episode is the narrative trigger for the final confession. Physical suffering produces the most extreme spiritual crisis in the book. The bodily pain of the stocks becomes the existential pain of the confession. This observation invites further reflection on the passage's contribution to the book's theological argument. The text operates on multiple levels simultaneously — historical, literary, and theological — and each level enriches the others when the passage is read with attention to its canonical context.

brueggemann.source: Walter Brueggemann, Jeremiah, ITC (1998) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Brueggemann reads the beating as the institutional response to prophetic truth: "When the temple establishment cannot refute the message, it assaults the messenger. Violence is the argument of last resort.". This observation invites further reflection on the passage's contribution to the book's theological argument. The text operates on multiple levels simultaneously — historical, literary, and theological — and each level enriches the others when the passage is read with attention to its canonical context.

hist.context: Pashhur son of Immer was the chief officer of the temple — effectively the head of temple security. His response to Jeremiah's sign-act (ch. 19) is physical violence: he has the prophet beaten and put in stocks at the Upper Benjamin Gate. This is the first instance of physical persecution recorded in the book, and it provokes both an oracle of judgment against Pashhur and the most intense of all the confessions (vv.7-18).

sections.header: Verses 7-18 — "Cursed Be the Day I Was Born"

heb.gloss: to deceive / to entice / to seduce
heb.paragraph: Jeremiah accuses God: "You deceived me (pittîtānî), LORD, and I was deceived" (v.7). The verb pātâ is strong — it can mean "to seduce" (as in Exod 22:16, seduction of a virgin) or "to entice" (as in 1 Kgs 22:21, the lying spirit). Jeremiah charges God with using superior power to lure him into an impossible vocation. The accusation is breathtaking in its intimacy and audacity.

Jeremiah curses the day of his birth (v.14): "Cursed be the day I was born!" This echoes Job 3:1-10 and inverts the prenatal calling of 1:5 — the prophet who was "known before birth" now wishes he had never been born. The arc from "before I formed you" (1:5) to "cursed be the day" (20:14) is the full trajectory of prophetic suffering.

refs.note: Job curses the day of his birth in nearly identical language — two suffering servants at the extremity of human endurance.
Paul's "jars of clay" passage echoes the paradox: the power of the message in the weakness of the messenger. "Always carrying in the body the death of Jesus."
"Before I formed you in the womb I knew you" — the prenatal calling that 20:14-18 desperately wishes to undo.

notes.note: "You deceived me" — Jeremiah accuses God of seduction. The prophetic call promised opposition (1:17-19) but not this level of suffering. The prophet feels trapped in a vocation he cannot escape.

"His word is in my heart like a fire, a fire shut up in my bones." The compulsion to prophesy is physical — the word burns within and must be spoken. Silence is impossible. The prophet cannot not speak.
An abrupt shift to trust: "The LORD is with me like a mighty warrior." This brief doxology (v.13: "Sing to the LORD!") seems to resolve the crisis — but it does not last.

The birth curse: "Why did I ever come out of the womb?" The confessions end with no answer, no resolution, no comfort. This is the darkest moment in prophetic literature — the prophet at the absolute bottom.

Calvin wrestles with the accusation of divine deception: "Jeremiah does not charge God with moral falsehood but with overwhelming power. God's call was irresistible, and Jeremiah feels used by a force he cannot control."

Calvin celebrates the fire metaphor: "The true minister cannot remain silent even when silence would be safer. The word of God is not an optional message but a compulsive force that must find expression."
Calvin admits the birth curse troubles him: "We must not take this as a model of prayer but as an honest record of human extremity. Even prophets reach the limit of endurance."

netbible.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: The verb pittîtānî is second person singular — Jeremiah addresses God directly in the intimate "you" form. The accusation is personal, not theological.
The phrase "fire shut up in my bones" (ʾēš ʿăṣūrâ bĕʿaṣmōtay) uses the root ʿṣr ("to restrain/confine"). The fire is imprisoned in the prophet's skeletal structure — it cannot be contained.

lundbom.source: Jack R. Lundbom, Jeremiah, Anchor Bible (3 vols., 1999–2004) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Lundbom identifies this as the sixth and final "confession" — the climax of the series. The progression across the confessions traces a descending arc: complaint (11:18) → theodicy (12:1) → birth-regret (15:10) → imprecation (18:21) → seduction charge and birth-curse (20:7-18). Each is more extreme than the last.

brueggemann.source: Walter Brueggemann, Jeremiah, ITC (1998) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Brueggemann calls this "the most daring prayer in Scripture." The prophet accuses God of using seductive power to trap him in an impossible vocation. Yet the very accusation is addressed to God — even in rage, the relationship holds.

Brueggemann reads the birth curse as "the collapse of prophetic identity." The confessions began with the prophet defending himself against external enemies; they end with the prophet turning against his own existence. There is no deeper crisis.

hist.context: Jeremiah 20:7-18 is the most intense personal lament in the Hebrew Bible. It divides into three movements: accusation and compulsion (vv.7-9), trust and praise (vv.11-13), and birth curse (vv.14-18). The juxtaposition of praise (v.13: "Sing to the LORD!") with the birth curse (v.14: "Cursed be the day!") is jarring and deliberate — faith and despair coexist without resolution. The book's confessions end here, at the bottom.

themes.note: Prophecy peaks at 10 — the confession is the most intense exploration of prophetic vocation in the Bible. The fire-in-bones passage defines the compulsion of divine calling.

rows.label: vv.7-9
rows.text: Final Confession: Fire in the Bones

rows.label: vv.10-13
rows.text: Abrupt Doxology: Sing to the LORD

rows.label: vv.14-18
rows.text: Birth Curse: Cursed Be the Day

lit.note: Chapter 20 pairs institutional persecution (vv.1-6) with the most extreme personal lament (vv.7-18). The confessions reach their nadir — fire in the bones, doxology, and birth curse juxtaposed without resolution.

trans.title: Comparing translations in Pashhur and the Final Confession

translations.text: And you, Pashhur, and all who live in your house will go into exile to Babylon. There you will die and be buried, you and all your friends to whom you have prophesied lies.’ ”

And you, Pashhur, and all who dwell in your house, shall go into captivity. To Babylon you shall go, and there you shall die, and there you shall be buried, you and all your friends, to whom you have prophesied falsely.”
You deceived me, Lord, and I was deceived; you overpowered me and prevailed. I am ridiculed all day long; everyone mocks me.
O LORD, you have deceived me, and I was deceived; you are stronger than I, and you have prevailed. I have become a laughingstock all the day; everyone mocks me.

thread.text: The apostles are flogged by the Sanhedrin — the pattern of religious authorities persecuting prophets continues into the NT.
Pashhur's successor Zephaniah is urged to put Jeremiah in stocks again — the institutional hostility persists across administrations.
Job curses the day of his birth in nearly identical language — two suffering servants at the extremity of human endurance.
Paul's "jars of clay" passage echoes the paradox: the power of the message in the weakness of the messenger. "Always carrying in the body the death of Jesus."
"Before I formed you in the womb I knew you" — the prenatal calling that 20:14-18 desperately wishes to undo.

coaching.tip: Pashhur strikes Jeremiah and puts him in stocks — the first physical persecution the prophet suffers. Jeremiah responds by renaming Pashhur: 'Magor-Missabib' — 'terror on every side' (v. 3). The name becomes the sentence. Like Isaiah naming his sons as prophecies, Jeremiah weaponizes language. The priest who tried to silence the prophet becomes a walking oracle of doom.

'Cursed be the day I was born!' (v. 14). Jeremiah echoes Job 3:3 almost verbatim. The prophet who delivers God's word wishes he'd never existed. This isn't faithlessness — it's the raw cost of faithfulness. Jeremiah doesn't leave; he doesn't stop prophesying; he doesn't recant. He just screams. The Bible preserves the scream alongside the sermon because both are part of the prophetic vocation.

prayer_prompt: As Jeremiah curses the day he was born yet cannot stop speaking God's word because it burns in his bones, consider asking God whether you have ever felt the tension of a calling you cannot escape even when it hurts.
